title: The Digital Age and Digitalisation
summary: Understanding the Digital Age: what digitalisation means for business, competitive advantage, and analytical frameworks like PEST, SWOT and Porter's Five Forces.
content_html:
The Digital Age and Digitalisation

The Digital Age refers to an era defined by data, digital devices, digital delivery, and digital demand. Understanding it is fundamental to business strategy.

Key Terminology

  • Digitisation: Converting text, pictures or sound into 0s and 1s processable by computers.
  • Digitalisation: Changing business processes driven by digital data and digital technologies.
  • Digital Transformation: The radical redesign, renewal, or development of a business driven by digital technologies.

The Five Ds of the Digital Age

  • Data: The most valuable asset -- the richest businesses in the world own it.
  • Delivery: Most services and transactions are fully digital.
  • Demand: Citizens expect ubiquitous internet connectivity.
  • Devices: Billions connected to the internet, dozens per person.
  • Dualism: We have dual (converging) digital and physical existences.

Technology Diffusion Theory

Adoption of a new innovation does not happen simultaneously. Early adopters, early majority, late majority, and laggards each adopt at different rates (Rogers, 2003). The Gartner Hype Cycle models maturity and adoption of emerging technologies through phases: Technology Trigger, Peak of Inflated Expectations, Trough of Disillusionment, Slope of Enlightenment, and Plateau of Productivity.

Business Opportunities and Threats

Digital transformation creates both opportunities and threats:

  • Opportunities: Exploit (new ways to do old things better), Rethink (faster and cheaper), Invent (new products altogether).
  • Threats: Inertia (failure to adapt), Replacement (new products compete), Competition (global marketplace).

Analytical Frameworks

PEST Analysis: Political, Economic, Social, Technological factors in the macro-environment.

SWOT Analysis: Strengths, Weaknesses (internal), Opportunities, Threats (external).

Porter's Five Forces: Competitive rivalry, Threat of new entrants, Threat of substitutes, Bargaining power of buyers, Bargaining power of suppliers.

exercises.title: Digitalisation definition
options: Changing business processes driven by digital technologies
exercises.answer: Changing business processes driven by digital technologies

exercises.title: Digital Age assets
exercises.prompt: In the Digital Age, what is considered the most valuable asset?

exercises.title: Hype Cycle phases
exercises.prompt: Which phase of the Gartner Hype Cycle comes AFTER the Peak of Inflated Expectations?

exercises.title: PEST category
exercises.prompt: A new government regulation requiring data localisation falls under which PEST category?

exercises.title: Porter's Five Forces
exercises.prompt: Porter's Five Forces does NOT include:

exercises.title: Digital transformation scope
exercises.prompt: Digital transformation differs from digitalisation because it involves:
options: Radical redesign and renewal of a business driven by digital technologies
exercises.answer: Radical redesign and renewal of a business driven by digital technologies

exercises.title: Diffusion of innovation
exercises.prompt: According to Rogers' diffusion of innovation theory, who adopts a new technology LAST?

exercises.title: Digital threat
exercises.prompt: Which is an example of a digital transformation THREAT to an existing business?
options: New ways to serve existing customers
New competitors entering via a global digital marketplace
exercises.answer: New competitors entering via a global digital marketplace
